topic.what: Review of chronic gastritis covering epidemiology, topographic distribution patterns (antral-predominant vs. corpus-predominant vs. pangastritis), natural history of mucosal atrophy progression, and clinical consequences.
topic.how: Expert narrative review synthesizing epidemiological studies, longitudinal cohort data on gastritis progression, and clinico-pathological correlations between topographic patterns and disease outcomes.
topic.why_matters: The topographic pattern of gastritis determines clinical risk — antral-predominant gastritis associates with peptic ulcer disease while corpus-predominant/pangastritis associates with gastric cancer risk — making topographic assessment essential for clinical management.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Correlation between gastritis topographic pattern and clinical outcome (ulcer vs. cancer risk)
characteristics.value: antral = ulcer risk; corpus/pangastritis = cancer risk
characteristics.vs_baseline: Reinforces established but often clinically underappreciated topographic-outcome associations
characteristics.context: H. pylori-associated chronic gastritis; topographic patterns may differ in autoimmune gastritis

good_for: Understanding the natural history and epidemiology of chronic gastritis
Risk stratification based on topographic distribution of inflammation and atrophy
Clinical decision-making regarding surveillance based on gastritis pattern
not_for: Non-H. pylori etiologies where topographic patterns differ
requires: Adequate biopsy sampling from both antrum and corpus (minimum Sydney protocol)

novelty: Comprehensive epidemiological synthesis linking topographic gastritis patterns to divergent clinical outcomes
Emphasizes the clinical importance of distinguishing antral from corpus-predominant disease for risk assessment
open_problems: Predicting which patients with antral-predominant gastritis will progress to pangastritis
Non-invasive biomarkers for topographic pattern assessment without biopsy
Interaction between H. pylori virulence factors and host genetics in determining topographic distribution
